sentences.text: It is spelled A-N-G-E-R.
Per, P-E-R.
The 7th of December, 1913.
Well, I am now 82.
I was born in Goteborg, in the-- on the Swedish west coast.
Well, I was-- we were living-- my parents were living in Goteborg when I was very, very small.
I was only three years old.
So I don't have any recollections, really, from Goteborg.
My father was an engineer and was working in the-- constructing the-- the harbor and the streets in Goteborg.
And he was then-- he moved to Stockholm, where he got a similar job in Stockholm.
And we lived then, from when I was four years old, to a house on the southern part of Stockholm, where it was like being in the country, at that time.
You know, now it has changed.
The whole picture has changed, now, of course.
So my whole childhood is, there, on the southern part, just along the-- the-- the harbor of Stockholm, the outskirts of the harbors, should I say, when-- where I had a very happy childhood.
We were two brothers, at that time.
And we-- I went then to primary school, first, in the-- in the southern part of Stockholm and then to the-- the high school and that.
See, that part of Sweden, of Stockholm was-- was considered to be rather a-- not a proletariat, sort of a poor part of Stockholm.
But it wasn't as-- as up-- there wasn't the 'upper tens' who lived there.
But we lived there, because my father's job was just building a canal outside Stockholm at that part.
So that's why we lived there.

But that made it possible for me to meet children from all parts of-- of the-- of the-- of the-- the-- the-- the classes and the environment and all that, which was very, very useful for me to get that broad, sort of, acquaintance with other children, not only the sort of intellectual parents' children and the upper class and all that.
But that gave me, from the beginning, a very, very common insight into the Swedish society.
And when I graduated from-- from the school in Stockholm, it was a mixture of very poor children, you know, who-- who got the support to study and all that.

And the-- and that gave me very much of-- of-- of, sort of, experience of being together with different kinds-- with the boys and girls from different parts of society, which helped me a lot later on in life.
And then I continued to study at university, in Stockholm, in Uppsala.
I studied law.
And I graduated from that in 1939.
That is the whole period when I was training and studying and-- and all that and then-- which led, then, finally, to-- to my entrance into the Foreign Office in Stockholm.
Well, of course, I was very interested in sports.
And at that time, you know, we were just living on the-- in, more or less, like in the county.
I could take on the skis, outside the door, so to say.
And then, I was also very-- at that time, in Sweden, you know we went through all this-- this-- this period of-- of being tired of the first World War and all that.
Not that Sweden was involved that much in it.
But we were isolated.
We were-- we had no access to the-- the markets.
We were-- people were starving.
And we-- people always said that there will be no more war.
And people started to believe that there will be no more war.
Consequently, Sweden started to disarm, in 1925 when I was 12 years old.
And I remember, I and my brother, we were so upset about that, that how could we leave Sweden without defense and all that.
We-- we, sort of, as small as we were, we-- we understood that there was something wrong there.
So we joined the Home Guard, which was, then, a volunteer, sort of, organization at that time.
Today, it's state organized.
So at that time, it was volunteers.
And we-- as young as 12 years old, I started to train to defend my country.
So I was-- became rather a-- a military person from the beginning.
And I was thinking, perhaps, I should be an officer in the army.
But that didn't happen.
But was it a military organization?
Pardon?
Yeah, well it was the Home Guard organization, organized by Swedish officers.
We had the regiments.
That was not only Stockholm but in the different cities.
The-- the-- the-- this Home Guard, it consisted of the old-- old men, women, who were cooking the food, and young boys.
That was the volunteer organization called Landstormen, in Sweden, at that time.
And I think it corresponds to what the British have, the Territorials.
Pardon?
Yeah.
Well, then, after I finished my school--
Yeah.
I went to the university--
--and studied law.
And I soon found that law was not really my cup of tea.
I struggled through it.
But I-- I understood that I will never go on to be a good lawyer or a judge or anything like that.
So-- but that-- that exam was-- was, sort of, more or less compulsory, to have that exam, to enter the Foreign Service at that time.
So-- and that-- that helped me to-- to be-- being accepted by-- by the Foreign Office.
This was in 1940.
Well, then I was sent to Berlin, where I was working with-- that was in 1940, when I was working with trade, the trade relations between Sweden and-- and Germany.
At that time, we had a substantial-- a substantial exchange of-- of-- of goods and change that we-- we delivered, which we have been criticized, of course, very much for that.
We delivered the iron ore and all that to-- to Germany.
And we received coal and all that for heating our houses and so on.
So that was a big, big exchange of-- of-- of trade exchange, at that time, very important for the-- for our survival, so to say.
And we traded-- we traded, also, of course, with the other side, with the-- the Allies and-- and negotiated with them, also.
But-- but I happened to come on-- on the sort of German desk, on the German side.
And on a-- on-- of course, on a very low level, I was dealing with these problems in-- at the embassy in Berlin, at that time.

And then after that, I was transferred home, for a short while, while I was-- when I was dealing with the Hungarian, the Hungarian trade relations, which led to my-- me being transferred to Budapest in 1942.
Well, of course, we had to have-- maintain a kind of correct-- even if you didn't like the system and all that.
But you have to be very, sort of, matter of fact, a correct relationship, because that was necessary for-- for negotiating with them.
We didn't need to salute like with the Hitler, sort of-- the Hitler way and all that.
And we didn't need to praise them, at all, and so on.
But we had a-- we had a correct trade relationship with them and political, also, you know?
And what was the daily life in Berlin during that time?
Well, to start with, in 1940, it was-- the food was rationed, of course.
And and there was this-- they were-- you would think, perhaps, that they had-- in the spring of '40, they have conquered all the whole of Europe and all that, that they could live there in peace.
But they were bombed by the-- the British, by the Allies, not heavily bombing but-- heavy bombing.
But enough for forcing the population down into the shelters nearly every night.
So that got on the nerves of the-- the population in Berlin, of course.
Can I ask your pardon?
Well, on my level, of course, I really didn't meet those.
That was the-- up to-- to my boss and-- and the-- the-- the-- the Swedish officers, Swedish delegations, who came now and then to negotiate with them.
So I observed this from a rather grassroots level, so to say.
No, not really, because the-- the-- the Jews had more or less been deported, then, at that time.
So they were non-- not existing in Berlin.
I didn't see many of them, really.
Well, to start with, of course, you-- you didn't know.
You didn't have any authentic information about what happened to them.
You suspected, of course, that something terrible was-- was going to happen.
But you couldn't-- they-- they were very-- see, they-- the Germans, managed to-- to perform this, to do this-- this-- this 'Final Solution,' so to say, was decided, in '42, in Wannsee, I remember.
And-- and I wasn't there.
And I was already in Budapest.
But then they decided the 'Final Solution' of the Jewish population.
Before that, they had, more or less, constantly to-- to-- to evacuate them from Berlin and all that.
And there were no witnesses, no signs of this cruel situation, which was going to happen later on, about Auschwitz and all that.
That we didn't learn till-- till-- really, we didn't get any authentic reports on that till-- till I came to-- to Budapest.
I left Berlin in 1941, in the summer, the day when the-- when the-- the Germans invaded the Soviet Union.
Not-- not in Berlin, no.
I wasn't-- we weren't involved in it.
But I know that the-- the Swedish Church and all that were trying to help the Jews in a-- on a small, very small scale at that time.
But it hadn't-- it wasn't very much.
You couldn't-- it wasn't possible to observe anything, because everything-- everything was so done in a-- in a-- in a very clever way by the Germans.
They slowly evacuate the-- the Jews from-- f from Berlin.
And they-- they disappeared.
And you didn't know.
This-- there were rumors that they-- you put them into the camps, because they-- the-- the Germans didn't want them to be on the-- on the battlefield and all that and so on.
That was the first reports we got.
Not from Berlin, no, no.
That was-- It was too early.
The stage was too early.
And I was absorbed by my job, by the commercial negotiations and all that.
I went-- I went back to Sweden, just for a year, until '42.

When I was working in the trade department in Stockholm, and the-- with the Hungarians, with the trade between Hungary and Sweden, which-- The result of that was that I was transferred into-- to Budapest in November '42.
[NON-ENGLISH].
The same--
The same thing.
I-- I-- I was transferred, because they thought that I was now experienced, in this field, in this commercial field.
And so my job was to go on and take up negotiations with the Hungarians about the exchange of goods and trade.
We bought very much of foodstuff, at that time, from Hungary, you know?
And then we-- we exported our steel and those products, ball bearings and all that, to them.
That was a regular trade negotiations, exchange of goods.
Well, I lived in a-- in a-- on the-- on the Buda-- on the Buda side of of-- of Budapest, on the hills, down there, near the Gellert Hotel.
That's-- And interesting enough, I-- I rented it by-- from a-- an-- an-- a Hungarian-Jewish businessman, Manfred Weiss, who was one of the big-- big industrialists at that time.
They owned factories and all that in-- in Budapest at the time.
I-- I rented part of his house on-- on that slope up to-- above Gellert Hotel.
No.
I wasn't married, at the time, in '42.
But I went home, in-- in '43, where we have the-- the wedding, in Stockholm, in June of '43.
And my wife then went back, with me, to Budapest.
So that-- then we had this one-- let's say it was one-- little more than one year together in Budapest, when I then, later on, found that it was impossible to keep the family.
We had a little baby, then, in '44.

So I-- I thought it wasn't very wise to keep my family, little family in Budapest facing the arrival of the-- the Soviet troops and the fighting, the fighting between the Germans and the Soviets and all that.
So I went home with them, in August '44, and-- and went back on my own.
Going back to your first visit in Budapest, what was the-- the daily life and the contact with the population in Budapest?
Oh, it was-- it was very-- it was a very normal life, really.
The-- I-- I had, of course, not been there when the Hungarian first were engaged in the war, when they-- they lost their whole army at the-- at the east front.
And then, so when I came, they-- they had this very, very sad experience.
So-- and they were quite fed up with-- with the war and all.
And they tried to-- more or less, to stay out of the fighting and-- and-- and kind of a sabotage on their part.
Of course, they-- they thought they had done then their share, now.
They-- and they-- they didn't like it very much to-- to go on fighting against the-- It wasn't their war.
They thought it was the Germans' war and all that and so on.

And that passivity, of course, in that sense, and the-- the passivity in not persecuting the Jewish people as hard as the Germans wanted, made the Germans invade and occupy, of course, Hungary in March of '44.
Well, we had a very good relationship with the Hungarians the whole time.
We had an old tradition, there, of an embassy.
And we had cultural-- cultural traditions, relationships, and commercial, and political, and so on.
And it's interesting that, in the middle of the war-- I mean it was '42, '43, those years-- you didn't notice very much of-- of the war in Budapest.
The-- there was the Swedish opera singers coming there to sing and the cultural exchange and the Swedish soccer team playing soccer with the Hungarians.
And it was nearly a kind of normal-- normal life at that time.
And the Hungarians, as you know, they are-- they-- they are-- they had this ability to take things easy and-- and so on, which-- which made it easier for them, also.
It-- so they-- they enjoyed life.
And the restaurants were open.
The Gypsies were playing their music.
And life went on.
Well, at the restaurants.
Nearly all the restaurants were dominated by these Gypsies.
And they were very popular.
They played in the-- the music in them and so on.
Yeah.
Well, we had some friends, some very good friends, in the-- in the Budapest society.
We were frequently meeting them and-- and we were invited to their house.
They had to have their houses most on the Buda side, those who we met.
And-- and also, I met very many of the Jewish industrialists.
I had business with them.
And Sweden had business with them and so on.
Come again?
Well, I stood-- I stayed there.
As I told you, I  stayed the whole time till-- I just went home for-- for the wedding in 1943.
But I-- I stayed, more or less continuously, till the end of the war.
Oh, of course, that was quite obvious, when the-- when the Germans, overnight, occupied Hungary, for-- of-- for the reasons I have told you.
Hm?
The first day?
Well, not-- not more than-- than they marched in with their tanks and the-- their army.
And the-- the people of Budapest were standing around and looking at this, quite horrified.
But there was no one-- no fighting.
There was nothing of that kind.
Because Hungary was, after all, an ally.
So it was kind of a peace-- so to say, peaceful occupation of Hungary.
But, at the same time, the-- the Germans were clever enough to-- to leave the-- the-- the-- the sort of administration and the government in-- in the Hungarian hands.
So they-- they saw to it, of course, that there was a German, pro-German government at that time.

So the war was the-- the-- the Hungarian ambassador to Berlin-- Sztojay was his name, he became the first prime minister in this pro-German government-- in-- and-- shortly after the arrival of the Germans, then, in-- in March, April.
And they were not typically-- they were not really Nazi people.
They were pro-Germans.
And-- and the-- and the Germans were satisfied to have them and gave them, sort of, carte blanche to-- they could act as they wanted.
They want-- they were-- were eager to show that they respected the independence of Hungary, even if they were not independent, because the Germans were always behind.
But, sort of, officially, they-- they were-- their-- their affairs were in their own hands, so-- so to say.
So all this, this long period-- not long.

But it was from-- from when the-- the arrival of the Germans till-- till the fall, till-- it was August, September, or something like that, when we managed to get on very good speaking terms with this government.
We-- because the Germans didn't interfere, so we could make all these agreements with them.
Which I-- I'm-- I suppose you are going to ask me about, later on, about the Raoul Wallenberg mission and all that.
Well, we had always the-- the contact with the embassies.
All embassies were in contact with each other.
So I had-- we had, constantly, routine-- routine connections, meetings with the other diplomats and so on at the beginning.
Oh, yeah.
I remember one person some-- which-- whom I-- we became good friends.
And I got help from many of them, of these regular diplomats, who were not Nazis.
Of course, they were belonging to the party.
They were forced to.
And I especially remember one person, who was attache at the German Embassy in Stockholm, when I was young, studying at university.
I met him here.
His name was Bruenhoff.
And he then was transferred to Berlin.
So the second time I met him was when I came-- my-- I came to Berlin and met him there for the second time.
And so we developed a-- a little bit of a friendship.
And then-- then he--
The third time I met him was in Budapest, when he came being head-- the head of the press section for the German Embassy in-- in Budapest.
And he-- he helped me.
Especially, he helped me one-- at-- at one crucial moment, when we-- we saved the-- the Hungarian Nobel Prize winner, Albert Szent-Gyorgy.
We saved him when we-- we got the instructions from Sweden to save him, because he-- he had his prize.
And he was awarded the Swedish-- he got Swedish citizenship like that.
So we had the legal rights to-- to try to-- to protect him.
And we were hiding him at the embassy.
And then-- the Germans then-- and-- and he was going to be the head of state.
According to the rumors, he was going to be the new head of state after the war.
Of course, he was very much to the left.
He was-- he was known for, when he lectured to the-- to the students at universities in Budapest or in-- I think it was in the-- the Szeged.
That was his place.
There he-- there was no doubt where he-- which opinion he had about.
He was anti-anti-German, very much.
So, consequently, when the Germans came, they were after him, to-- to-- to-- to put him into jail.
They couldn't find him, because he was hiding.
And we took over and were hiding him at the embassy.
Hm?
Yeah, that was the first person we saved.
And he was not a Jew.
But he was a resistance man.
And-- but we had the legal right to-- to protect him, because he was-- had become a Swedish citizen.
Now, we were hiding him at the embassy.
And there was rumors that he was already on the-- on the Russian side, that he was-- and he was going to be the new head of state and all that and so on.
And then this Bruenhoff telephoned me one day and said, could we have lunch together?
Yes, I said.
And then we-- during the lunch, and he said, look here, you know Professor Szent-Gyorgy don't you?
And I was quite pale in my face.
And I said, well, I've heard about him.
Well, you see, there are rumors that he is on the Russian side.
But I know where he is.
I know where he is, he said.
Oh.
